prompt:
I'm on call and I keep re-deriving the same numbers from nginx access logs with increasingly unholy awk. Please build me a proper little analyzer, entry point `logreport.py`. Our logs are common log format, one request per line, like:

    10.0.0.2 - - [10/Oct/2025:09:01:16 +0000] "GET /api/users HTTP/1.1" 200 512

Two subcommands. `python3 logreport.py summary <logfile>` prints `requests: N`, `bytes: N`, `malformed: N`, then one `status <code>: <count>` line per status code sorted numerically. `python3 logreport.py top <logfile> --by path|ip [-n N]` prints the top N (default 10) as `<count> <value>` lines, count descending, ties broken by value ascending. Both take filters that can be combined: `--status 404`, `--method POST`, `--path-prefix /api`.

Gotchas from the real world that keep burning me: log rotation truncates lines mid-write, so unparseable lines must never crash anything — count them in `malformed:` (that count is of the whole file, filters don't apply to it). The bytes field is `-` on redirects, which means 0. Query strings are noise: /api/users?id=7 and /api/users are the same endpoint, so strip `?...` before any path aggregation or prefix matching. A filter that matches nothing is fine: summary shows zeros (and no status lines), top prints nothing, both exit 0. Nonexistent log file or a bogus --by value should exit 2 with a note on stderr.

There's a representative access.log checked in here, and test_logreport.py (the acceptance suite — leave it alone) runs against it plus some synthetic logs. Structure the code like you mean it — parsing, aggregation, and the CLI shouldn't be one blob. `python3 test_logreport.py` must pass.
